title: Ideas & Experiments
text:
This section gathers applied projects, tools, and experiments at the intersection of data science, development economics, and public policy. The work here is exploratory — combining satellite imagery, geospatial data, and statistical methods to address concrete policy problems in developing country contexts.



  
    
      
        SPATIAL ANALYSIS
        Togo · 2024
      
      
        Development of Spatial Targeting Tools for Togo
      
      
        A data-driven composite priority index combining census, satellite, and geospatial layers to rank cantons and neighborhoods by vulnerability — supporting the allocation of social and infrastructure investments.
      
      Read more →

title: Research
text:
Publications
Revisiting the Environmental Kuznets Curve: Evidence from West Africa with Yao Silvère Konan Environmental Economics, Vol. 12(1), 2021, pp. 64–75 DOI: 10.21511/ee.12(1).2021.06


Abstract

This paper analyzes the revenue-pollution relationship by revisiting the Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC) hypothesis for West African countries over the period of 1980–2014. The study approximates the income measurement by GDP per capita and uses carbon dioxide (CO2), nitrogen oxide (NO2), and methane emissions as various environmental quality measures. The paper uses parametric and non-parametric estimation techniques to test the EKC hypothesis. The results support the existence of the U-inverted relationship between income and methane emission, on one hand, and between income and nitrogen dioxide emission on the other. The estimates also show a mixed result for the U-inverted hypothesis between income and carbon dioxide emissions. Thus, the verification of the curve depends on the estimation techniques and the measurement of the pollutant used. The obtained results led to the conclusion that the EKC hypothesis is validated for West African countries.


Impact du Conseil Agricole sur les Performances des Producteurs d’Anacarde de Côte d’Ivoire with Doukouré Charles Fé European Scientific Journal, Vol. 14(30), 2018, p. 292 DOI: 10.19044/esj.2018.v14n30p292


Abstract

The cashew sector has been booming in Côte d’Ivoire for the last three years. In the face of international competition, it appeared important to improve the performance of producers in order to maintain the comparative advantages of Côte d’Ivoire. In this perspective, the agricultural advisory project was set up to train cashew farmers in good practices. This study aims to evaluate the impact of this project on their performance in terms of returns. The data used for the analysis come from the survey conducted by the National Agency for Rural Development (ANADER). The average effect was estimated through the propensity score matching method by using the kernel method. The results reveal that the advice and training support of cashew producers has led to a significant increase in yield of 22.4% on average.

title: Development of Spatial Targeting Tools for Togo
text:
Spatial Analysis
  Public Policy
  Remote Sensing
  Togo · 2024

title: Development of Spatial Targeting Tools for Togo
text:
Introduction
Effective targeting of social and infrastructure investments requires more than aggregate poverty statistics — it demands a fine-grained understanding of where vulnerabilities concentrate, overlap, and interact across space. In contexts where administrative capacity is limited and resources are scarce, identifying priority territories is both a technical and a political challenge.
This project develops a data-driven territorial targeting tool for Togo designed to support evidence-based allocation of public investments. The tool integrates population and poverty information from the 2022 national census with a rich set of satellite-derived and geospatial indicators. These inputs are combined into a composite priority index that ranks all cantons nationwide and all neighborhoods (quartiers) in Greater Lomé according to the intensity of overlapping vulnerabilities.
The objective is operational: to provide policymakers, development agencies, and local governments with a transparent, reproducible framework for identifying areas where interventions are likely to generate the greatest social impact — moving beyond ad hoc targeting toward a structured, data-grounded approach.

title: Development of Spatial Targeting Tools for Togo
text:
Methodology

  Core principle: The index aggregates multiple vulnerability dimensions into a single ranked score, allowing direct comparison across territorial units at two scales — canton (national) and neighborhood (Greater Lomé).

The construction of the priority index follows four main steps:
1. Unit of analysis
The tool operates at two complementary scales:

Canton level — 117 cantons nationwide, enabling national-level prioritization of rural and peri-urban territories.
Neighborhood (quartier) level — fine-grained targeting within Greater Lomé, where spatial heterogeneity is high and aggregate statistics mask localized deprivation.

2. Indicator normalization
Each raw input is transformed into a normalized score on a common 0–1 scale using min-max normalization, so that higher values consistently indicate greater vulnerability or need. For indicators where higher raw values reflect better conditions (e.g., wealth index, road accessibility), the score is inverted.
3. Composite index construction
Normalized indicators are aggregated into a weighted composite score. Weights reflect the relative policy relevance of each dimension and are calibrated through stakeholder consultation and sensitivity analysis. The resulting index provides a continuous vulnerability score for each territorial unit.
4. Ranking and classification
Units are ranked from highest to lowest priority score and classified into quintiles for visualization and operational use. The top quintile identifies territories where multiple deprivations overlap — the primary targets for investment.

title: Development of Spatial Targeting Tools for Togo
text:
Inputs
The index integrates seven categories of indicators, combining administrative, satellite, and geospatial data sources:


  
    👥
    Population & Poverty
    Population counts, poverty headcount ratios, and household welfare estimates from the 2022 RGPH census and the EHCVM household survey.
  

  
    🌙
    Nighttime Lights
    NASA VIIRS nighttime light radiance as a proxy for economic activity and electrification. Low luminosity signals infrastructure deprivation.
  

  
    💰
    Relative Wealth Index
    Meta / Facebook Relative Wealth Index — a high-resolution satellite-based estimate of relative asset wealth at 2.4 km grid level.
  

  
    🏥
    Service Accessibility
    Travel time to the nearest health facility, school, and market, computed from OpenStreetMap and road network data using friction surfaces.
  

  
    🛣️
    Road Network
    Road density and quality classification from OpenStreetMap, capturing physical connectivity and isolation of territorial units.
  

  
    🌊
    Flood Risk
    Flood hazard exposure derived from the Global Flood Database (GFD) and FATHOM hydrological models, identifying climate-vulnerable zones.
  

  
    🏘️
    Built-up Density
    Human settlement layer from the Global Human Settlement Layer (GHSL), measuring built-up area density as an indicator of urbanization pressure.

title: Development of Spatial Targeting Tools for Togo
text:

  Results coming soon
  
    This section will present canton-level and neighborhood-level priority maps, quintile distributions, and sensitivity analyses. Results are currently being finalized.

title: Kodjo Aklobessi
text:
I am a third year PhD student in Economics at the Université de Montréal. My research lies at the intersection of Family Macroeconomics, Labor Economics, and Applied Microeconometrics.
I have over three years of professional experience working with international and national statistical and development institutions, including the World Bank, AFRISTAT, and INSEED. My work has focused on household survey design, poverty measurement, social protection targeting systems, and applied policy analysis in developing country contexts. Through these roles, I have developed strong expertise in large-scale data collection, econometric analysis, and the translation of empirical research into policy-relevant insights.
